title: Ensure That IAM Users Are Not Assigned the Service  Account User or Service Account Token Creator Roles at Project  Level

input_row.title: Ensure That IAM Users Are Not Assigned the Service  Account User or Service Account Token Creator Roles at Project  Level
input_row.description: It is recommended to assign the Service Account User (iam.serviceAccountUser) and Service Account Token Creator (iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator) roles to a user for a specific service account rather than assigning the role to a user at project level.
input_row.rationale: A service account is a special Google account that belongs to an application or a virtual machine (VM), instead of to an individual end-user. Application/VM-Instance uses the service account to call the service's Google API so that users aren't directly involved. In addition to being an identity, a service account is a resource that has IAM policies attached to it. These policies determine who can use the service account. Users with IAM roles to update the App Engine and Compute Engine instances (such as App Engine Deployer or Compute Instance Admin) can effectively run code as the service accounts used to run these instances, and indirectly gain access to all the resources for which the service accounts have access. Similarly, SSH access to a Compute Engine instance may also provide the ability to execute code as that instance/Service account. Based on business needs, there could be multiple user-managed service accounts configured for a project. Granting the iam.serviceAccountUser or iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator roles to a user for a project gives the user access to all service accounts in the project, including service accounts that may be created in the future. This can result in elevation of privileges by using service accounts and corresponding Compute Engine instances. In order to implement least privileges best practices, IAM users should not be assigned the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level. Instead, these roles should be assigned to a user for a specific service account, giving that user access to the service account. The Service Account User allows a user to bind a service account to a long-running job service, whereas the Service Account Token Creator role allows a user to directly impersonate (or assert) the identity of a service account.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the IAM page in the GCP Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. Click on the filter table text bar, Type Role: Service Account User. 3. Ensure no user is listed as a result of the filter. 4. Click on the filter table text bar, Type Role: Service Account Token Creator. 5. Ensure no user is listed as a result of the filter. From Google Cloud CLI To ensure IAM users are not assigned Service Account User role at the project level: gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json | jq '.bindings[].role' | grep "roles/iam.serviceAccountUser" gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json | jq '.bindings[].role' | grep "roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" These commands should not return any output.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the IAM page in the GCP Console by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam. 2. Click on the filter table text bar. Type Role: Service Account User 3. Click the Delete Bin icon in front of the role Service Account User for every user listed as a result of a filter. 4. Click on the filter table text bar. Type Role: Service Account Token Creator 5. Click the Delete Bin icon in front of the role Service Account Token Creator for every user listed as a result of a filter. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Using a text editor, remove the bindings with the roles/iam.serviceAccountUser or roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator. For example, you can use the iam.json file shown below as follows: { "bindings": [ { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", ], "role": "roles/appengine.appViewer" }, { "members": [ "user:email1@gmail.com" ], "role": "roles/owner" }, { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", "serviceAccount:123456789012-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" ], "role": "roles/editor" } ], "etag": "BwUjMhCsNvY=" } 2. Update the project's IAM policy: gcloud projects set-iam-policy PROJECT_ID iam.json
input_row.impact: After revoking Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level from all impacted user account(s), these roles should be assigned to a user(s) for specific service account(s) according to business needs.
input_row.default_value: By default, users do not have the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator role assigned at project level.
input_row.additional_information: To assign the role roles/iam.serviceAccountUser or roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator to a user role on a service account instead of a project: 1. Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/projectselector/iam- admin/serviceaccounts 2. Select Target Project 3. Select target service account. Click Permissions on the top bar. It will open permission pane on right side of the . Add desired members with Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator role.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit process for this control can be fully automated using GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), and GCP CLI commands. 
- The audit mentions specific commands using gcloud CLI to check for violations, implying that these features are accessible programmatically.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_iam_project_service_account_roles_assignment_restrictions

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json` command to retrieve the IAM policy of the project.
- The automated program needs to check for the roles "roles/iam.serviceAccountUser" and "roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" within the policy.
- Enumerate all the bindings in the project's IAM policy to find any entries that contain the specified roles.
- The expected configuration should ensure that there are no IAM user assignments for these two roles at the project level.
- **Pass Criteria:** The program should confirm that the commands do not return any output for both roles, meaning no user has been assigned these roles at the project level.
- Any presence of these roles in the output indicates a failure, thereby suggesting the need for remediation following the provided steps in the audit control.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The task of checking IAM roles can be effectively automated using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` command is appropriate for retrieving the IAM policy and checking for specific roles at the project level.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly accurate. The `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json` command is valid for retrieving the IAM policy in JSON format, which can then be parsed to check for the presence of specific roles.
- The description of the pass criteria is clear, and the method for checking roles is technically sound.
- The program name, `gcp_iam_project_service_account_roles_assignment_restrictions`, is descriptive but could be improved for clarity and adherence to naming conventions.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be more descriptive. A suggestion would be `gcp_iam_project_check_service_account_roles_at_project_level` to clearly indicate that the check is for service account roles at the project level.
- Consider adding a step to log the results of the checks for audit purposes, which could be useful for compliance tracking.
- It might be beneficial to include a check for any service accounts that might have these roles at the project level, not just IAM users, to ensure comprehensive coverage.
- An alternative method could involve using the IAM Policy Analyzer in GCP, which provides a more granular analysis of IAM policies and could complement the gcloud command approach.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-founded in terms of using GCP SDK capabilities and the gcloud command for automation. The technical details are mostly correct, and the approach aligns with best practices for automating IAM role checks.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name to `gcp_iam_project_check_service_account_roles_at_project_level` for better clarity and convention adherence.
- Consider including additional checks for service accounts and logging for audit trails.
- Overall, the automated approach should be retained as it effectively leverages GCP's capabilities for compliance auditing.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the audit process for this control can be automated using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` command allows for programmatic access to the IAM policies, enabling the detection of specific roles assigned at the project level. This capability makes automation feasible and efficient for ensuring compliance with the control requirements.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_iam_project_check_service_account_roles_at_project_level

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** IAM Roles
- **API/CLI Method:** `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json`
- **Properties to Check:** Bindings for roles "roles/iam.serviceAccountUser" and "roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator"
- **Pass Criteria:** No IAM user assignments for these roles at the project level
- **Fail Criteria:** Presence of these roles assigned to any IAM user at the project level

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
There were no significant disagreements between the AI opinions. Both agreed on the feasibility of automation and provided similar technical details, which I have synthesized into a final decision.
